At this stage, I expect increased price competition as brands are finding it difficult to stay ahead via innovation. This means that over a longer horizon, premium brand margins will erode as budget brands find it easier to produce the quality previously only seen in brands like Nike. As long as the world keeps subscribing to a free-trade approach, and production is sourced at the lowest dollar-value manufacturing, we will keep seeing a deflationary trend. Alternatively, if countries move in a direction to protect domestic labor, then we will see jumps in pricing, which will initially hurt gross margins.

While the estimate for the whole footwear market is between $460B and $490B, I calculated the relative peer market share from a total revenue base of $103.6B in the last 12-months, and $96.1B in 2022. We can see that relative to their peers, Nike and Adidas lost the most market share, while Deckers and Skechers gained the most. The gain for SKX is likely explained by the value approach for their brand, while DECK is likely gaining momentum from their UGG and Hoka brands that grew 16% and 23.7% respectively in Q3.

I expect the trend to continue, and incumbent market share to be eroded by value footwear as well as novelty premium brands like Deckers & On Holding (ONON).

Latest Results & Outlook

For the full-year 2024, Skechers reported revenue of $8.97B, up by 12.1% vs 2023. The largest regional driver was EMEA, up 21%. Wholesale led growth with 13.2% vs direct to consumer (DTC) with 10.7% growth. The gross margin for wholesale is 43.3%, while DTC has a higher 66.2% gross margin, hence why growth in this channel is more important for the value of the company.

Over the last 3 years, SKX had a revenue CAGR of 12.4%, resulting from a stable expansion from DTC and EMEA.

On the expense-side, SKX has a $496M increase in operating expenses on a $615M increase in gross profit. Most of the increased expenses are related to administrative expenses, and can likely be explained by the 2.1K increase in total employees to 13.6K. This means that the company is spending more on growth and was a net employer in 2024. The operating margin is 10%, which indicates room for improvement, as peers like Nike and Deckers have operating margins of 12% and 23.5%, respectively.

The net income attributable to common shareholders is $640M, resulting in a margin of 7.2%, slightly above the 6.8% margin in 2023. The company repurchased $330.1M worth of stock in 2024, reflecting a 3.3% yield.

For 2025, SKX forecasts revenues around $9.75B, representing an 8.3% growth over 2024. EPS is forecasted around $4.4. The company is expecting an income tax around 22.5% and total capex between $600M and $700M, including investments in U.S. and China distribution centers (warehouses).

Intrinsic Value At $12 Billion

Regarding future revenues, I am modeling a declining growth curve that starts at management’s guidance of 8.5% and slowly converges on 4%. This leads to my revenue forecast of $18B in 2035, representing a doubling from the current $9B revenues and a market share of 2.6% on a total industry value of $695B in 2035. Compared to my 2024 industry value estimate of $475.5B, SKX’s market share would increase from 1.9% to 2.6%.

Note, in order to get my $695B industry value for 2035 I took the projected $580B midpoint for 2030 from Statista and GVR, and extrapolated a 3.7% CAGR. Conversely, I get the 2024 industry value as the midpoint between Statista’s $494B and GVR’s $457B 2024 estimates.

I estimate an intrinsic value of $12B for Skechers or $80 per share. This is my base case (you can tweak the model), which assumes a declining revenue growth curve over time, as well as business efficiency improvements reflected as increases in the operating margins from 10% to 12% in the next 3 years.

My base case also assumes an efficiency penalty, reflected as a high need for reinvestment. While management has delivered robust top-line growth over the last few years, I am not confident in their ability to convert the top-line into value for shareholders, hence why I am modeling net reinvestment between $680M and $830M which covers everything from increases in working capital to net capex.

From my model above (click to enlarge), you can notice that most of the present value comes from the terminal value at $10B, while only $3B comes from the free cash flows in the next 10 years. This isn’t a “bad” circumstance, as it means that the company’s value will increase over the years before settling into its market fit. It is also an appropriate gauge for investors’ expectations regarding cash returns such as buybacks, i.e., I expect low buybacks until the company scales to $1B in operating income.

Risks

  • Skechers may be negatively impacted by tariffs on China, which is their major production hub that exports to the U.S. A mild tariff scenario will decrease margins, while high tariffs will force high capex spending to relocate production.

  • The company may continue running at a relatively lower efficiency, resulting in stagnant margins. This would limit the value creation and would make revenue growth a neutral factor for the value of SKX.

  • The company is facing design patent infringement litigation from Nike, with a trial date set to 15 September 2025. They are also facing an appeal to the litigation alleging misuse of corporate assets (jets). In my view, even if SKX ends up getting the short end, I expect a long trial process, and potential impacts to be covered over time.

data.title: Global Water Resources: High Revenue And Margin Growth Priced In
data.description:
Global Water Resources (NASDAQ:GWRS) (TSX:GWR:CA) is a water utility that provides water, recycled water, and wastewater services in Maricopa County, Arizona. In recent years, this geographic area has experienced a demographic and economic boom due to the opening of numerous manufacturing sites by several major US and international companies.

This expansion has allowed GWRS to increase active connections at a 7.2% CAGR since January 2019, reaching a total of 64,000 as of September 2024, which was also achieved through several acquisitions. The resulting increase in revenue has enabled operational scaling, leading to a steady improvement in operating margins and profitability.

Moreover, the regulated nature of the company's business provides an excellent inflation hedge, making GWRS a low-volatility stock with good growth prospects. Nevertheless, the DCF analysis performed within the article indicates an overvaluation, with a potential downside of approximately 15%. For this reason, I assign Global Water Resources a “hold” rating.

Business Overview

GWRS is a water management company operating 32 water systems across the Phoenix and Tucson areas, with a total of 64,000 active connections as of September 2024, up 4.7% YoY. Its primary freshwater supply comes from the Maricopa-Stanfield Sub-basin aquifer, which has a total capacity of about 23,000 acre-feet of water per year. GWRS's operations are divided into 2 segments:

The Water Services segment supplies fresh water directly from groundwater and accounted for 49.15% of revenue in 9m FY24, up 2.48% YoY. During this period, 72.2% of the segment's revenue came from residential customers, up 5.59% YoY, while the remainder was generated by commercial operations, up 17.3% YoY, and agricultural operations, down slightly compared to 9m FY23.

The Wastewater and Recycled Water Services segment focuses on wastewater disposal and recycling and accounted for 50.85% of revenue in the first 9m of FY24, up 5.8% YoY. Residential customers remain the largest source of revenue in this segment, contributing 92.8%, while recycled water revenue accounted for just 6%.

Arizona Demographic Boom

In recent years, Arizona (particularly the area around Phoenix) has attracted billion-dollar investments from chip manufacturers such as TSMC and Intel, automotive companies, and tech firms, turning the state into a nationwide data center hub. Moreover, the extensive business activity surrounding these companies has indirect effects, such as attracting their suppliers and boosting local businesses, creating a multiplier effect on job demand. The Arizona Commerce Authority estimated $50 billion in CAPEX in 2024 alone, generating 24,251 jobs. Additionally, the Arizona Office of Economic Opportunity projected the creation of 477,968 new jobs between 2022 and 2032.

These developments should enable GWRS to increase the number of both residential and commercial customers, an expansion that can be sustained organically. The company currently uses only 38% (8,750 acre-feet) of its allocated portion of the Maricopa-Stanfield Sub-basin aquifer's annual capacity, allowing it to potentially serve 40,000 additional homes.

Growth Strategy

Management is executing an aggressive growth strategy that has allowed the company's revenue to increase at a CAGR of 11.1% between FY20 and FY23, driven by three pillars: new service areas, acquisitions, and utility rate increases.

Since FY17, GWRS has completed 12 acquisitions, adding a total of 7,670 connections and over $4 million in revenue. In March 2024, the company announced plans to acquire seven public water systems from the City of Tucson for $8.4 million in cash, adding about 2,200 new connections and expanding its presence in the region. The transaction is expected to close by early 2025. These and other recent transactions highlight how this sector, often operated by public entities, suffers from low operational efficiency due to high fragmentation. In this context, an acquisition-based strategy enables sector consolidation, increasing the scalability of operations and positively impacting the company's margins.

Finally, GWRS management has planned several rate cases. In June 2024, the ACC approved one in the Saguaro Region, resulting in a positive revenue impact of $0.4 million starting in Q3 FY24. In July 2024, filings were initiated for the Farmers' Water utility, requesting a revenue increase of about $1.1 million, which was approved in February FY25. A rate case for two of the company's largest utilities is planned for H1 FY25, with eventual approval expected in 2026; the company has not provided an estimated amount for this revenue increase at this time.

Financial Snapshot

In 9m FY24, GWRS revenue decreased slightly by 3%. Despite this, excluding the one-off revenue of $2.79 million reported in the first 9m FY23, the company achieved a 4.1% YoY growth. EBIT margin and net income margin decreased compared to 9m FY23, again due to the one-off revenue, but showed an increasing trend over the period under analysis between FY20 and 9m FY24.

For FY25, I expect a 10% increase in revenue, driven by both rate cases and the acquisition of the City of Tucson facilities, as well as an increase in active connections due to population growth in the region. Similar dynamics are expected for FY26. Revenue growth should also lead to a slow but steady improvement in margins, supported by stable or slightly increasing fixed costs.

On the balance sheet side, GWRS reported net debt of $108 million as of September 2024, resulting in a Net Debt/EBITDA ratio just over 4x and an interest coverage ratio of 1.76x in the first 9m FY24, confirming relatively low financial risk. In addition, the company continues to generate positive OCF aligned with CAPEX, resulting in minimal capital requirements from external sources despite a rather aggressive growth strategy. In my opinion, at a more mature stage, lower CAPEX should lead to higher FCF generation, which would help reduce debt and increase shareholder remuneration, currently modest with a dividend yield (FWD) of 2.66%.

Discounted Cash Flow

To attribute a valuation to GWRS, I performed a three-stage DCF. I made an estimate of the expected values of revenue, EBIT margin, CAPEX, and depreciation for FY25, FY26, FY27. I made a projection of these values for the period between FY28 and FY34, finally calculating the terminal value. This analysis returned a target price of $9.78 per share, which is about 15% lower than GWRS current market price. To determine this value, I made the following assumptions:

Beta was obtained using the unlevered beta of "Utility (Water)" industry reported by Professor Aswath Damodaran, subsequently calculating levered beta based on GWRS D/E ratio. A value of 0.61 was derived.

Risk-free rate of 4.50%, based on the US 10-year treasury yield.

Equity risk premium of 4.33% was obtained from US ERP provided by Aswath Damodaran. Cost of equity of 6.16% derived from a CAPM model.

Cost of debt of 4.91%.

WACC of 6.16%.

The growth rate (g) of 2%.

Main Risks

GWRS operates in a highly regulated industry, and changes in regulation can have a major impact on the company.

The specific nature of the business makes GWRS sensitive to climate change and environmental damage.

Operating exclusively in Arizona, GWRS has low geographic diversification, making its economic performance dependent on the health of the state's economy.

The aggressive growth strategy implemented by management may lead to further increases in debt, which, in a high-interest rate environment, could result in lower profitability.

Takeaway

Global Water Resources operates in a geographic area characterized by high population growth, a factor that, combined with the aggressive growth strategy implemented by management, I believe will lead to a gradual increase in revenue and margins in the coming years, as a result of consolidation in the highly fragmented and inefficient water market. Although the level of debt is quite significant, the interest coverage ratio of 1.67x indicates financial health, with the possibility of using debt to finance growth that cannot be funded through OCF. While I haven't identified any particular risks in the company's business model, I believe it is slightly overvalued, as evidenced by the DCF, which indicates a potential 15% downside. For these reasons, I assign GWRS a “hold” rating.
